text: The video depicts a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy for carcinoma of the cecum. The procedure begins with the mobilization of the mesenteric root at the ligament of Treitz, incising the peritoneum anterior to the fourth part of the duodenum. The mesenteric root is retracted to expose the uncinate process of the pancreas, revealing the superior mesenteric vein. The dissection plane is extended to mobilize the right-sided mesocolon along the mesocolic fascia. The mesenteric root and ileocolic vessels are exposed, and the meso is incised at the angle between these structures. The superior mesenteric vessels are encountered peripheral to the ileocolic vessels' origin. Lymphadenectomy is performed along the mobilized mesenteric route, isolating and dividing the ileocolic vein at its origin, with access to the ileocolic artery due to the mesenteric root's rotation.

The surgeon performs an en bloc lymphadenectomy along the superior mesenteric artery, avoiding the nerve plexus, up to the pancreas. The transverse colon is retracted cranially, and the mesocolon is incised to open the lesser sac between the right and left branches of the middle colic artery. The lines of incision of the mesocolon and mesenteric root are connected, and the origin of the right branch and middle colic artery are dissected. In cases where the branches of the middle colic artery are not visible, the incision is made anterior to the superior mesenteric vein to prevent injury.

The surgeon performs a lymphadenectomy for complete mesocolic excision, dissecting down to the right aspect of the middle colic artery. The lymphatic tissue of the right branch of the middle colic artery is kept en bloc with the lymphatic tissue of the superior mesenteric vessels. The dissection of the veins of the gastro-mental trunk of Heinle is shown, facilitated by the previous exposure of the pancreatic head and lesser sac structures. The superior right colic vein is divided at its confluence with the gastro-mental vein and the ascending superior pancreaticoduodenal vein.

The surgeon completes the division of the gastrocolic ligament and omentum at the site of the future colon division, leaving the gastroepiploic lymph nodes in place. The right colon is fully mobilized en bloc with the mesocolon. The colon is exteriorized through a short supraumbilical laparotomy, followed by an extracorporeal resection and side-to-side anastomosis. The specimen displays an intact mesocolon and peritoneal window framed by lymphatic tissue, indicating a complete mesocolic excision.
